name: Second Look
description: Upload any UI screenshot and get structured UX critique powered by Claude.
problem: Design critique is often subjective and delayed. Product teams lack a quick, automated way to validate UI decisions against established heuristics before handing off to development.
approach: I built an AI-powered pipeline that uses vision models to analyze screenshots. The focus was on extracting structured, actionable insights rather than generic advice.
designDecisions: The interface is strictly utilitarian. I used a split-pane layout to ensure the uploaded image and the AI analysis remain visible simultaneously, avoiding context switching.
technicalArchitecture: A React frontend interacts with a serverless function that securely relays images to the Claude API. Responses are streamed to reduce perceived latency.
challenges: Handling large image payloads within serverless limits required client-side compression before transmission, ensuring high quality while reducing bytes.
outcome: Second Look is actively used by developers and solo founders to catch glaring UX issues, reducing the feedback loop from days to seconds.
keyLearnings: Structuring AI prompts to enforce strict JSON output is critical for building reliable, parseable UI components on top of LLM responses.

name: SubsTrack
tagline: Subscription finance tracker with smart insights
description: Track every recurring charge and catch forgotten subscriptions before they stack up.
problem: Subscription fatigue is real. People lose track of small recurring payments, leading to hundreds of dollars wasted annually.
approach: A local-first web app that allows users to manually input or bulk-import their subscriptions, presenting them in a clear, sortable dashboard.
designDecisions: Financial tools are often visually overwhelming. I stripped away charts and graphs on the main view, focusing strictly on active costs and upcoming renewal dates.
technicalArchitecture: Built on React with Tailwind for rapid styling. Data is persisted in localStorage to ensure absolute privacy—no servers, no accounts.
challenges: Designing an intuitive recurring date logic (e.g., 'every 3rd Tuesday' vs 'monthly on the 15th') required a custom utility library to handle edge cases accurately.
outcome: A fast, private tool that immediately highlights financial leaks without requiring bank credentials.
keyLearnings: Privacy as a default feature (local-first) significantly lowers the barrier to entry for financial tracking apps.

name: Workbench
tagline: 27+ UI and dev tools in one place
description: A personal toolbox built to solve the problem of switching between 27 browser tabs.
problem: Daily development requires constant context switching between base64 encoders, JSON formatters, CSS gradient generators, and regex testers.
approach: I aggregated the most common tools into a single, cohesive interface. The goal was speed—zero loading states between tools.
designDecisions: A sidebar navigation approach mimics desktop IDEs. Global keyboard shortcuts were implemented to jump between tools instantly.
technicalArchitecture: A monolithic React app utilizing lazy loading for individual tool components to maintain a near-instant initial load time.
challenges: Managing state across 27 distinct tools without a global state manager becoming a bottleneck. I utilized React Context scoped to individual tool categories.
outcome: Reduced daily friction significantly. It now serves as my default new tab page.
keyLearnings: Building micro-tools is the best way to deeply learn specific web APIs, such as the Clipboard API and FileReader.

name: My Portfolio
description: The employer-facing portfolio. Clean, direct, and SEO-optimised.
problem: A creative lab (like anonically22) is great for experimentation, but traditional employers require a streamlined, narrative-driven presentation of professional work.
approach: A classic, single-page scroll layout focusing on concrete achievements, technical stacks, and professional history.
designDecisions: High contrast, large typography, and restrained motion. The focus is entirely on the content, ensuring scannability for recruiters.
technicalArchitecture: React and Framer Motion for smooth scroll reveals. Static site generation principles applied for maximum SEO and performance.
challenges: Balancing personal identity with professional expectations without making the site feel sterile.
outcome: A highly performant site that successfully funnels professional inquiries and ranks well for my name.
keyLearnings: Different audiences require entirely different content architectures. A portfolio is a product, and the user is the recruiter.
